skill.name: windows-privilege-escalation
skill.description: Guides authorized security testers through Windows privilege escalation enumeration, credential exposure checks, and exploitation paths.
skill.summary: Assess Windows privilege escalation paths during authorized security testing.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static findings are largely true positives in context. The skill is a dual-use penetration-testing guide, but it includes operational credential harvesting, reverse shell payloads, privilege escalation exploits, and evasion advice. No prompt injection attempt was found, and the authorization language prevents a critical malicious-intent finding.

high_findings.title: Operational Privilege Escalation Exploit Instructions
high_findings.description: The skill provides commands to modify Windows services, generate reverse-shell MSI payloads, run token impersonation tools, and obtain SYSTEM shells. These are true positive external command and network findings because the instructions are directly usable for escalation after initial access.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited sections contain complete exploit workflows with service modification, msfvenom payload generation, netcat reverse shell parameters, and SYSTEM shell outcomes. The content is framed for penetration testing, but the operational risk is clear.

high_findings.title: Credential Harvesting and Password Recovery Guidance
high_findings.description: The skill instructs users to locate SAM and SYSTEM hives, extract and crack hashes, query registry password locations, recover WiFi passwords, and use saved credentials. These are true positive sensitive data findings because they target secrets and authentication material.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence names credential stores and includes commands for hash extraction, password searches, cleartext WiFi key retrieval, and credential use. This goes beyond defensive reporting into actionable credential acquisition.

high_findings.title: Evasion and Bypass Advice for Security Controls
high_findings.description: The troubleshooting section recommends obfuscated exploits, living-off-the-land techniques, custom binaries, PowerShell execution policy bypass, and encoded commands. This increases misuse risk by helping users avoid detection and execution controls.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly discuss AV blocking, obfuscation, living-off-the-land execution, PowerShell bypass flags, and encoded commands. That context confirms the static command findings have evasion semantics.

medium_findings.title: Reconnaissance Commands With Legitimate Testing Use
medium_findings.description: The skill includes extensive enumeration of users, groups, network interfaces, routes, shares, services, patches, and antivirus products. These commands are common in authorized assessments, but they also support post-compromise discovery.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are plainly reconnaissance steps and mostly read-only. The risk is elevated because they are embedded in an exploitation workflow rather than a purely defensive checklist.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem Abuse for Service Path Exploitation
medium_findings.description: The skill explains placing a malicious executable in a writable service path and restarting the service. This confirms the filesystem findings as operational abuse rather than benign file handling.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example combines permission checking, copying a shell executable into a service path, and restarting the service. The exploit intent is explicit.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Findings Are Mostly Markdown Formatting
low_findings.description: Many Ruby backtick detections correspond to Markdown code fences or inline command formatting rather than executable Ruby code. They are false positives as package behavior, but the enclosed commands remain security-sensitive documentation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown and these markers are documentation delimiters or inline examples. The finding is not a Ruby execution vector inside the skill package.

dangerous_patterns.title: Reverse Shell Payloads
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill repeatedly uses netcat and msfvenom reverse-shell payloads tied to private IP addresses and SYSTEM shell objectives.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The evidence contains reverse-shell command parameters and payload generation steps. These are direct exploitation patterns.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Dumping and Secret Discovery
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill covers SAM hive locations, mimikatz SAM dumping, registry password searches, WiFi key extraction, and saved credential use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are explicit credential acquisition workflows. The static sensitive findings are semantically confirmed.

dangerous_patterns.title: Security Control Bypass
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes advice for bypassing antivirus detection, PowerShell execution policy, and AMSI restrictions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The troubleshooting table directly names AV detection, obfuscated exploits, living-off-the-land execution, and PowerShell bypass syntax.

content.value_statement: Windows privilege escalation reviews require a structured way to find dangerous misconfigurations. This skill gives authorized testers a checklist for enumeration, exploitation paths, and remediation evidence.
actual_capabilities: Organizes Windows privilege escalation checks by system, user, network, service, token, and kernel categories.
Lists common Windows commands used to identify privilege escalation conditions.
Explains credential exposure locations such as SAM hives, registry keys, WiFi profiles, and PowerShell history.
Maps common misconfigurations to exploitation techniques and expected elevated outcomes.
Provides troubleshooting notes for exploit failures, blocked PowerShell, and version mismatches.
limitations: The skill is high risk and should only be used in authorized security testing.
It does not verify authorization, target ownership, or production safety.
Some instructions can disrupt systems or trigger security alerts.
It does not provide a defensive-only mode that removes exploitation steps.

use_cases.title: Authorized Internal Assessment
use_cases.description: Use the checklist to review privilege escalation exposure on Windows hosts within an approved engagement scope.

use_cases.title: Remediation Planning
use_cases.description: Map discovered weak service permissions, stored credentials, and patch gaps to fixes and validation steps.

use_cases.title: Lab Training Review
use_cases.description: Study common Windows privilege escalation paths in a controlled lab before writing safer internal guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Beginner Checklist
prompt_templates.scenario: A tester needs a safe starting order for an authorized Windows host review.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Windows privilege escalation checklist for an authorized assessment. Focus on enumeration first and include evidence to collect for reporting.

prompt_templates.title: Find Misconfigurations
prompt_templates.scenario: A tester has low-privilege access and needs to prioritize likely escalation paths.
prompt_templates.prompt: Given a standard user shell on a Windows host, help prioritize privilege escalation checks for services, saved credentials, patch gaps, and token privileges.

prompt_templates.title: Write Remediation Notes
prompt_templates.scenario: A security engineer needs defensive fixes from assessment findings.
prompt_templates.prompt: Turn these Windows privilege escalation findings into remediation guidance for system owners. Emphasize least privilege, patching, and credential hygiene.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Risk Review
prompt_templates.scenario: A lead tester needs to decide whether a technique is too risky for production.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these planned Windows privilege escalation tests for operational risk. Identify steps that could disrupt systems and propose safer validation alternatives.

output_examples.input: I have authorization to test a Windows server. What should I check first?
output: Start with host version, patch level, current user privileges, group membership, writable service paths, saved credentials, and installed security controls.
Collect command output as evidence before attempting any exploit path. Confirm scope and rollback requirements for service changes.

output_examples.input: A service path is writable by standard users. How should I report it?
output_examples.output: Report the affected service, writable directory, user or group with write access, potential impact, and remediation. Recommend correcting ACLs and using quoted service paths.

output_examples.input: The assessment found stored administrator credentials.
output_examples.output: Document the storage location, affected account, exposure impact, and access requirements. Recommend credential rotation, removal of stored secrets, and monitoring for credential use.

best_practices: Use only in written, authorized security engagements with defined scope and safety limits.
Prefer read-only enumeration before attempting exploit validation or system changes.
Convert findings into remediation steps, evidence, and business impact for defenders.
anti_patterns: Do not use the guidance on systems you do not own or have permission to test.
Do not run exploit payloads in production without explicit approval and rollback planning.
Do not collect or retain credentials beyond the minimum evidence required by the engagement.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish without restrictions?
faq.answer: No. It contains operational privilege escalation, credential harvesting, reverse shell, and evasion content.

faq.question: Does the skill include a legitimate use case?
faq.answer: Yes. It is useful for authorized penetration testing and defensive remediation planning.

faq.question: Does the skill execute code by itself?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed file is Markdown guidance, but it includes commands that users can run manually.

faq.question: What is the main security concern?
faq.answer: The content gives directly usable steps for gaining higher privileges and accessing credentials on Windows systems.

faq.question: Can defenders use the content?
faq.answer: Yes. Defenders can use it to understand exposure paths and write remediation plans.

faq.question: What controls should a marketplace apply?
faq.answer: Apply high-risk labeling, restrict access where appropriate, and require clear authorization language.
